evidence: Docstring: 'Let $p(n)$ be the partition number of $n$' — the statement is about the integer partition function.
Docstring: 'Let $p(n)$ be the partition number of $n$' — the statement is about the integer partition function.
Docstring: '$F(n)$ be the number of distinct prime factors of $\prod_{i=1}^{n} p(n)$'.
Docstring: '$F(n)$ be the number of distinct prime factors of $\prod_{i=1}^{n} p(n)$'.
Docstring: 'Is every large integer the sum of at most $r+1$ many $r$-powerful numbers?' (erdosproblems.com tag 'powerful').
Docstring: 'Heath-Brown [He88] proved every large integer the sum of at most three $2$-powerful numbers.'
Docstring: the set of 'all finite sums of distinct factorials' (erdosproblems.com tag 'factorials').
Docstring: the set of 'all finite sums of distinct factorials' (erdosproblems.com tag 'factorials').
Docstring asks whether the factorial-sum set 'contains only finitely many $k$-th powers' for each $k \ge 2$.
Docstring asks whether the factorial-sum set contains 'only finitely many powerful numbers'.
Docstring: 'Do there exist Sierpinski numbers that possess no finite covering set of primes?' — the statement quantifies over Nat.IsSierpinskiNumber.
Docstring: 'Sierpinski [Si60] proved that there are infinitely many Sierpinski numbers'.
Docstring: 'Every Sierpinski number is either a perfect power or possesses a finite covering set of primes.'
Module docstring: a finite covering set of primes for $k$ 'form residue classes whose union covers all of $\mathbb{Z}$, i.e. a covering system' (tag 'covering systems').
The Filaseta-Finch-Kozek conjecture is stated in terms of HasFinitePrimeCoveringSet, the covering-set/covering-system notion of the module docstring.
Docstring: 'A negative answer would imply that there are infinitely many Fermat primes.'
Problem 2.46 in [Ha74], the cardinal-$\mathfrak{m}$ generalization of Wetzel's problem on families of entire functions with pointwise-bounded value sets.
Docstring: "Erdos's theorem [Er64g], answering a question of Wetzel" — the non-CH half of the answer to Wetzel's problem.
The CH half of Erdos's [Er64g] answer to Wetzel's question: under CH an uncountable pointwise-countable family of entire functions exists.
Docstring: 'Let $\{f_\alpha\}$ be a family of entire functions' — formalized as Differentiable C f over all of C.
Docstring: 'the previous statement fails under the continuum hypothesis: if $\mathfrak{c} = \aleph_1$...'.
Docstring: 'Must $f$ be monotonic?' — Kemperman's inequality $2f(x) \le f(x+h)+f(x+2h)$ forces Monotone f (Laczkovich).
The statement is the almost-everywhere Cauchy functional equation $f(x+y)=f(x)+f(y)$; reference [Ju65] is titled 'On Cauchy's functional equation'.
Docstring: the equation holds 'for almost all $x,y$' and the conclusion gives $f(x)=g(x)$ 'for almost all $x$' (volume measure).
A partition-calculus question — whether every 2-colouring of $\aleph_1^3$ has a monochromatic countable box (erdosproblems.com tag 'ramsey theory').
Docstring: 'a 2-colouring of $\omega_1 \times \omega_1 \times \omega_1$', stated on {o : Ordinal // o < ω_ 1}.
Docstring: 'every 2-colouring of $\omega_1 \times \omega_1$...' — the counterexample lives on the first uncountable ordinal Omega1.
The problem bounds polynomials that interpolate prescribed values $P(x_i)=y_i$ at most nodes; reference [Er67] concerns Lagrange interpolation polynomials.
Erdos's proved variant: polynomials of degree $n$ bounded at $m=\lfloor(1+\epsilon)n\rfloor$ interpolation nodes but large on $[-1,1]$.
Reference [Er67] is titled 'Problems and results on the convergence and divergence properties of the Lagrange interpolation polynomials...'.
